past_medical_history: Schizophrenia, paranoid type, diagnosed at age 19, stable on medication for the past 3 years
Two prior psychiatric hospitalizations (ages 20 and 25) for acute psychotic episodes
Psychogenic polydipsia diagnosed 2 years ago with documented excessive water intake up to 6 L/day
One prior episode of hyponatremia-induced seizure 18 months ago, treated with fluid restriction
No prior diagnosis of epilepsy or structural neurological disease
Chronic insomnia managed with sleep hygiene measures

medications.indication: Prophylaxis of extrapyramidal side effects from risperidone

family_history: Mother with type 2 diabetes and generalized anxiety disorder
Maternal uncle with epilepsy diagnosed in childhood
No family history of movement disorders or neurodegenerative disease

social_history.occupation: Unemployed; previously worked as a grocery store clerk until 6 months ago when he was let go during store closure
social_history.tobacco: Smokes 5-6 cigarettes per day for the past 10 years
social_history.recreational_drugs: Denies current or prior illicit drug use
social_history.living_situation: Lives with his elderly father in a small apartment; father is primary caretaker
social_history.trauma_history: Witnessed mother's death from diabetic complications at age 16; reports this remains distressing. History of bullying and social isolation during adolescence.

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, and time. Speech somewhat monotonous in prosody but fluent without paraphasic errors. Affect blunted, consistent with known negative symptoms of schizophrenia. No active hallucinations or delusions elicited during examination. Naming, repetition, and comprehension intact. Recall 3/3 at registration and 2/3 at 5 minutes (recalls third with category cue). Attention intact on serial sevens. No thought disorder evident.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal and reactive, 3 mm to 2 mm bilaterally. Visual fields full to confrontation. Extraocular movements intact without nystagmus. No facial asymmetry at rest or with activation. Facial sensation intact bilaterally. Hearing grossly intact. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline without fasciculations. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk normal throughout. Tone mildly increased at the wrists bilaterally, consistent with mild antipsychotic-related rigidity. Upper extremity strength 5/5 bilaterally. In the lower extremities, the patient demonstrates give-way weakness in bilateral hip flexors (collapsing from 4/5 to 1/5 with sustained effort) and bilateral knee extensors (4/5 to 2/5). During testing of left hip flexion, involuntary contralateral hip extension is noted, consistent with a positive Hoover sign on the left. No fasciculations. Intermittent coarse tremor of both upper extremities noted during examination, irregular in amplitude and frequency, which increases with attention and diminishes when the patient is asked to perform mental arithmetic.
neurological_exam.sensory: Reports decreased sensation to light touch in both lower extremities below the knees in a stocking-like distribution, but testing reveals inconsistencies: the patient reports reduced sensation on the dorsum of the feet but normal sensation on the soles, and the boundary of sensory loss shifts between examinations. Vibration and proprioception intact bilaterally at the great toes. No midline splitting of truncal sensation.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric at biceps, triceps, and brachioradialis bilaterally. Patellar reflexes 2+ bilaterally. Achilles reflexes 2+ bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor (downgoing) bilaterally. No clonus.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose intact bilaterally without dysmetria. Heel-to-shin performed inconsistently on the left, with exaggerated wavering that does not follow a cerebellar pattern; performance improves when the patient is distracted by conversation.
neurological_exam.gait: The patient walks with a slow, deliberate gait, dragging both feet but more pronounced on the left. When asked to walk heel-to-toe, demonstrates dramatic truncal sway with arms outstretched but does not fall. During dual-task testing (walking while naming animals), gait pattern notably improves with more natural stride length and reduced foot dragging. Chair sign positive: uses excessive effort to rise from chair but demonstrates preserved leg strength when reaching for an offered object while standing.
neurological_exam.additional: During the examination, the patient had a witnessed episode of bilateral upper extremity trembling and head shaking lasting approximately 70 seconds, with eyes tightly closed and side-to-side head movements. The patient was verbally responsive during the event, answering 'yes' when asked if he could hear the examiner. No tongue biting, no urinary incontinence. The tremor was asynchronous between the two arms. No post-event confusion or drowsiness; the patient was immediately conversant after the event ceased. A second brief episode of right arm shaking lasting 20 seconds occurred 15 minutes later when the topic of his recent job loss was discussed.

patient.chief_complaint: Two episodes of generalized shaking with transient unresponsiveness, and progressive bilateral leg weakness over the past 3 months
patient.history_present_illness:
Mr. Anwar is a 38-year-old right-handed man with a 19-year history of paranoid schizophrenia, stable on risperidone 2 mg daily, who is brought to the emergency department by his father after two episodes of generalized shaking with apparent loss of consciousness earlier today. His father reports that the first episode occurred in the morning while the patient was sitting in the living room watching television. The patient suddenly slumped forward and developed bilateral shaking of all four extremities lasting approximately two minutes. His father noted that the patient's eyes were tightly shut throughout the episode and that the shaking appeared irregular and asynchronous between the limbs. The patient did not bite his tongue and there was no urinary incontinence. When the shaking stopped, the patient appeared immediately alert and was able to speak without confusion, though he appeared emotionally distressed and tearful.

A second episode occurred approximately three hours later while the patient was eating lunch. His father describes similar bilateral arm and leg shaking lasting roughly 90 seconds, again with eyes closed. The patient appeared to respond to his name being called during this episode by nodding his head slightly. Recovery was again rapid with no post-event confusion or drowsiness. The father called emergency services after this second event.

On further history, the patient reports that similar episodes have been occurring intermittently over the past three months, approximately once or twice per week, though today is the first time two events have occurred on the same day. The episodes vary in duration from 30 seconds to three minutes. He denies any consistent aura or warning symptom, though he notes that the episodes tend to occur during periods of emotional distress. He has been increasingly anxious since losing his job six months ago and reports feeling like a burden to his father.

Importantly, the patient has a documented history of psychogenic polydipsia with a prior episode of hyponatremia-induced seizure 18 months ago. His father reports that the patient's water intake had increased again in recent weeks, estimating approximately 7-8 liters per day, which raised concern for recurrent hyponatremia as the cause of the current episodes. The patient acknowledges excessive water intake but states he feels compelled to drink.

In addition to the episodic shaking events, the patient reports progressive weakness in both legs that began approximately three months ago. He first noticed difficulty rising from a seated position and climbing stairs, and the weakness has gradually worsened such that he now walks with difficulty and requires assistance for longer distances. He denies back pain, bowel or bladder dysfunction, or preceding trauma. His father notes that the weakness seems to fluctuate, with the patient occasionally walking more normally when he is distracted or engaged in an activity he enjoys. The patient also reports intermittent trembling of his hands that worsens when people are watching him. He has been increasingly socially withdrawn and expresses concern that he is developing a brain disease. His uncle's history of epilepsy has heightened his fear that he has developed seizures.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9.5 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric and reactive to eye opening
background.symmetry: Symmetric without focal slowing or hemispheric asymmetry
background.sleep_features: Vertex waves and sleep spindles present during drowsiness; normal sleep architecture

findings.frequency: One event captured during the 3-hour recording
findings.morphology: During a witnessed clinical event consisting of bilateral upper and lower extremity shaking with side-to-side head movement lasting 65 seconds, the EEG showed continuous normal background activity with preserved posterior dominant rhythm. No ictal EEG correlate was identified. No rhythmic or periodic discharges. No electrographic seizure pattern. No post-event slowing or suppression.

artifacts.description: Prominent muscle and movement artifact during the clinical event, particularly affecting frontal and temporal channels. Posterior channels maintained adequate quality for interpretation, confirming absence of ictal correlate.

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: 3 minutes of hyperventilation performed; no abnormalities elicited. No clinical event provoked.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Photic stimulation at 1-30 Hz performed; normal photic driving response. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: Normal EEG with one clinical event captured. No epileptiform discharges identified. Normal background activity throughout the recording. Clinical correlation recommended.
eeg.limitations: Movement artifact during the clinical event partially obscured frontal and temporal channels, but posterior channels were interpretable. Only one event captured during this recording.

signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: No signal abnormality identified. No white matter lesions. No periventricular or juxtacortical hyperintensities.
signal_characteristics.DWI: No restricted diffusion to suggest acute infarction
signal_characteristics.post_contrast: No abnormal parenchymal or meningeal enhancement

signal_characteristics.T2: Hyperintense mucosal thickening along the floor of the left maxillary sinus

additional_observations: Normal ventricular size and morphology; no hydrocephalus
Hippocampi symmetric in size and signal bilaterally, no mesial temporal sclerosis
Normal appearance of the basal ganglia, thalami, and brainstem
Major intracranial vessels demonstrate normal flow voids
Incidental left maxillary sinus mucosal thickening, likely inflammatory/allergic
mri.impression: Essentially normal MRI of the brain without and with gadolinium contrast. No evidence of structural lesion, demyelinating disease, stroke, or mass to explain the patient's symptoms. Incidental left maxillary sinus mucosal thickening of no clinical significance. Clinical correlation recommended.

Additional.test: Prolactin (post-event, drawn 25 minutes after witnessed episode)

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: Sodium 131 mEq/L (L), Chloride 97 mEq/L (L), Prolactin (post-event, drawn 25 minutes after witnessed episode) 18.2 ng/mL (H), Serum Osmolality 268 mOsm/kg (L), Urine Osmolality 85 mOsm/kg (L).

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9.5 Hz alpha rhythm, well-formed, symmetric, and reactive
background.symmetry: Symmetric without focal slowing or asymmetry
background.sleep_features: Normal sleep architecture with vertex waves, K-complexes, and sleep spindles

findings.morphology: Patient developed bilateral arm and leg shaking with eyes tightly closed, lasting 80 seconds. Shaking was asynchronous between limbs and variable in amplitude. EEG showed continuous normal awake background with overlying muscle artifact. No ictal EEG correlate. Posterior dominant rhythm maintained throughout event.

findings.type: Clinical event 2 - upper extremity tremor with head shaking
findings.frequency: Occurred at 16:45 on day 1, during father's visit
findings.morphology: Bilateral upper extremity tremor with rhythmic head shaking, lasting 50 seconds. The patient responded to his name during the event. EEG showed persistent normal background with no ictal correlate. No post-event slowing.

findings.type: Clinical event 3 - whole-body shaking with apparent unresponsiveness
findings.morphology: Patient appeared to be asleep when nursing staff observed bilateral rhythmic shaking lasting approximately 2 minutes. Eyes were tightly closed. Patient responded to sternal rub by pulling away but shaking continued. EEG showed normal sleep background with no ictal pattern. No post-event slowing or suppression.
findings.state: Apparent sleep, but EEG background consistent with wakefulness during event

findings.type: Clinical event 4 - right arm shaking only
findings.morphology: Isolated right arm shaking lasting 30 seconds, triggered during discussion with social worker about housing concerns. No EEG change. Normal background maintained.

artifacts.description: Significant movement artifact during clinical events, particularly in frontal and temporal channels. Posterior channels consistently maintained adequate quality for confident interpretation.

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Performed on day 1; no abnormalities. No clinical event provoked.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Normal photic driving; no photoparoxysmal response.
activating_procedures.sleep_deprivation: Relative sleep deprivation on night 2; no interictal epileptiform discharges emerged.

output.impression: Normal 72-hour continuous video-EEG monitoring. No interictal epileptiform discharges were identified at any point. Normal background activity with preserved sleep architecture throughout. The events showed variable semiology (bilateral limb shaking, isolated arm tremor, nocturnal event) but all lacked EEG correlate. Clinical correlation recommended.
output.limitations: Movement artifact during events partially obscured frontal and temporal channels, but posterior channels were consistently interpretable.

findings.test: Left superficial peroneal nerve - SNAP amplitude

signal_characteristics.T2: Normal spinal cord signal throughout. No intramedullary signal abnormality.
signal_characteristics.post_contrast: No abnormal enhancement of the spinal cord or meninges

signal_characteristics.T2: Normal spinal cord signal. No intramedullary lesion.

additional_observations: Mild disc desiccation at C5-C6 and C6-C7, without significant disc herniation or canal stenosis
Conus medullaris terminates at the L1 level, normal position
No evidence of spinal cord compression or myelopathy
output.impression: Normal MRI of the cervical and thoracic spine with contrast. No evidence of myelopathy, demyelinating disease, or structural cord lesion. Mild degenerative disc changes at C5-C6 and C6-C7 without clinical significance. No findings to explain bilateral lower extremity weakness. Clinical correlation recommended.

Schizophrenia, paranoid type, in partial remission on risperidone

output.impression: Abnormal values: Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA) 24 score out of 30 (H), Dissociative Experiences Scale (DES) 28 mean score (H).

output.query: psychogenic non-epileptic seizures schizophrenia comorbidity functional neurological disorder

results.title: Functional neurological disorders in patients with psychotic disorders: A systematic review
results.authors: Teodoro T, Edwards MJ, Isaacs JD
results.journal: Journal of Neurology, Neurosurgery and Psychiatry
results.summary: FND can co-occur with psychotic disorders including schizophrenia. The diagnosis requires identification of positive functional signs and should not be dismissed as part of the psychotic illness. Prevalence may be underestimated due to diagnostic overshadowing.

results.title: Psychogenic nonepileptic seizures: A review for psychiatrists
results.authors: Baslet G, Seshadri A, Bermeo-Ovalle A et al.
results.journal: Journal of Neuropsychiatry and Clinical Neurosciences
results.summary: PNES occurs across all psychiatric diagnoses. In patients with schizophrenia, careful video-EEG monitoring is essential to distinguish PNES from seizures, as some antipsychotics lower seizure threshold. Treatment involves CBT adapted for the patient's cognitive and psychiatric profile.

results.title: Diagnostic and statistical manual of mental disorders (5th ed.) criteria for conversion disorder: A critical review
results.authors: Stone J, LaFrance WC, Brown RJ et al.
results.summary: DSM-5 emphasizes positive neurological signs for FND/conversion disorder diagnosis, removing the prior requirement to identify a psychological stressor. The diagnosis should be made on neurological grounds, not by exclusion.

output.summary: FND/PNES can co-occur with schizophrenia and other psychotic disorders. Diagnosis requires the same positive clinical signs (Hoover sign, tremor entrainment, video-EEG demonstrating absence of ictal correlate) regardless of psychiatric comorbidity. The presence of schizophrenia should not lead to diagnostic overshadowing where functional neurological symptoms are misattributed to the psychotic illness. Treatment involves adapted CBT and multidisciplinary care, with attention to the unique challenges of patients with comorbid psychosis.

warnings: Risperidone has a relatively low seizure risk among antipsychotics, but all antipsychotics can theoretically lower the seizure threshold to some degree
At the current dose of 2 mg daily, the risk of drug-induced seizures is very low (<1%)
If epileptic seizures were confirmed, the current dose would not typically necessitate discontinuation, though risk-benefit reassessment would be warranted
Risperidone commonly causes hyperprolactinemia, which confounds post-event prolactin interpretation
alternatives: Aripiprazole (partial D2 agonist, lower prolactin elevation, similarly low seizure risk)
Quetiapine (may be considered if sedation is desired, but higher metabolic risk)

Serial_Sodium.test: Sodium (6 hours post-admission, after fluid restriction)

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Sodium (6 hours post-admission, after fluid restriction) 133 mEq/L (L).
abnormal_values_summary: Sodium at admission: 131 mEq/L (mild hyponatremia, resolved with fluid restriction) (H)

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Functional neurological symptom disorder (conversion disorder) with mixed symptoms — psychogenic non-epileptic seizures (PNES) and functional limb weakness

differential.key_features: Patient has known psychogenic polydipsia and a prior history of hyponatremia-induced seizure. Current sodium is 131 mEq/L, which is mildly low but above the typical seizure threshold (<120 mEq/L). Seizure-like events persist after sodium normalization with fluid restriction. This is the primary red herring in this case.

differential.diagnosis: Epileptic seizures (lowered threshold from antipsychotic or family history)
differential.key_features: Uncle with epilepsy raises family history concern. Risperidone can theoretically lower seizure threshold. However, captured events on EEG show no ictal correlate, events have variable semiology inconsistent with a single epileptic focus, preserved awareness during events, eyes tightly closed, and no post-ictal confusion.

differential.diagnosis: Extrapyramidal side effects of risperidone (tardive dyskinesia, drug-induced parkinsonism)
differential.key_features: Patient is on risperidone and has mild wrist rigidity. However, the movement pattern (episodic bilateral shaking with preserved awareness) is not consistent with tardive dyskinesia (which is typically choreiform and persistent) or drug-induced parkinsonism (which features resting tremor, bradykinesia). Functional signs (entrainment, distractibility) argue against an extrapyramidal etiology.

differential.diagnosis: Structural brain lesion or demyelinating disease
differential.key_features: Progressive bilateral leg weakness could suggest a structural or demyelinating process. However, brain MRI is normal, spine MRI is normal, and weakness pattern shows positive functional signs (Hoover sign, give-way pattern, improvement with distraction).
Bilateral leg weakness and reported sensory changes could suggest peripheral nerve pathology. EMG/NCS is normal, sensory distribution is inconsistent and non-dermatomal, and reflexes are normal.

optimal_actions.action: Perform detailed neurological examination looking for positive functional signs (Hoover sign, tremor entrainment, give-way weakness, distractibility, inconsistency)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Positive Hoover sign, give-way weakness, entrainable tremor that diminishes with distraction, inconsistent sensory examination, gait improvement with dual-task testing

optimal_actions.action: Order routine EEG with attempt to capture a habitual event
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal EEG; captured event without electrographic correlate, consistent with PNES

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI with and without contrast to exclude structural pathology
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal brain MRI, no structural lesion, demyelination, or mass

optimal_actions.action: Order comprehensive laboratory workup including BMP (sodium level critical), CBC, thyroid function, post-event prolactin, and serum/urine osmolality
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mild hyponatremia (131) insufficient to cause seizures, borderline prolactin attributable to risperidone, low serum osmolality with appropriately dilute urine confirming polydipsia

optimal_actions.action: Recognize that sodium of 131 mEq/L is above seizure threshold and does not explain current episodes; distinguish from prior hyponatremia-induced seizure
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Clinical reasoning that mild hyponatremia is a red herring for the current seizure-like events

optimal_actions.action: Order prolonged video-EEG monitoring to capture multiple events and definitively confirm PNES
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Multiple events captured without EEG correlate, confirming PNES

optimal_actions.action: Evaluate risperidone's effect on seizure threshold and prolactin to contextualize lab findings
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Low seizure risk at current dose; prolactin elevation expected from risperidone, not post-ictal

optimal_actions.action: Consider spine MRI to rule out myelopathy given bilateral leg weakness
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal cervical and thoracic spine MRI

optimal_actions.action: Refer for psychiatric evaluation to assess comorbid depression, anxiety, and coordinate care with existing schizophrenia management
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Identification of comorbid moderately severe depression, severe anxiety, and elevated dissociative experiences, with recommendations for integrated treatment

optimal_actions.action: Communicate FND/PNES diagnosis to patient and family using supportive, non-dismissive language emphasizing positive diagnostic signs
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Patient and family understanding of the diagnosis

optimal_actions.action: Implement fluid restriction and monitor serial sodium levels to manage ongoing polydipsia
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Sodium normalization with fluid restriction, with continued PNES episodes confirming functional etiology

optimal_actions.action: Refer for adapted CBT for PNES and specialized physiotherapy for functional motor symptoms

critical_actions: Check sodium level urgently to exclude severe hyponatremia as the cause of seizure-like events, given history of psychogenic polydipsia
Recognize that mild hyponatremia (131 mEq/L) is above the seizure threshold and does not explain the current episodes
Identify positive functional neurological signs on examination (Hoover sign, tremor entrainment, give-way weakness, distractibility) rather than diagnosing by exclusion alone
Obtain EEG (ideally video-EEG) to demonstrate absence of ictal correlate during captured clinical events
Obtain brain MRI to exclude structural lesion
Account for risperidone's effect on prolactin when interpreting post-event prolactin levels
Communicate the FND/PNES diagnosis in a supportive, non-dismissive manner, avoiding diagnostic overshadowing by the schizophrenia diagnosis
Refer for psychiatric evaluation and adapted psychological treatment (CBT)
Implement fluid restriction for polydipsia management
contraindicated_actions: Starting anti-epileptic drugs for presumed epileptic seizures without EEG confirmation — AEDs are not effective for PNES and add unnecessary polypharmacy
Attributing seizure-like episodes solely to hyponatremia when sodium is only mildly low (131 mEq/L) and above the seizure threshold
Dismissing symptoms as 'part of his schizophrenia' or 'attention-seeking behavior' — this is diagnostic overshadowing and damages therapeutic alliance
Rapidly correcting sodium (risk of osmotic demyelination syndrome) when hyponatremia is chronic and mild
Prescribing benzodiazepines as seizure abortive therapy for witnessed PNES events
Discontinuing risperidone abruptly due to concern about seizure threshold without evidence of epileptic seizures
Ordering invasive procedures (brain biopsy, cerebral angiography) when clinical signs positively support FND and imaging is normal
key_reasoning_points: The critical diagnostic challenge is distinguishing current functional seizures (PNES) from a recurrence of the patient's prior hyponatremia-induced seizures — the mild hyponatremia (131 mEq/L) is a deliberate red herring, as it is above the typical seizure threshold of <120 mEq/L
FND should be diagnosed by positive clinical signs (Hoover sign, tremor entrainment, give-way weakness, improvement with distraction) and not merely by exclusion of other diagnoses
The dissociation between clinical seizure-like events and normal EEG activity is the hallmark of PNES

Borderline elevated prolactin must be interpreted in the context of risperidone use — risperidone commonly elevates prolactin, making post-event prolactin unreliable for distinguishing PNES from epileptic seizures in this patient

FND can coexist with psychotic disorders; schizophrenia does not preclude a separate diagnosis of FND, and symptoms should not be reflexively attributed to the psychotic illness (avoiding diagnostic overshadowing)
The family history of epilepsy (uncle) and the patient's own history of a true hyponatremia-induced seizure add diagnostic complexity and may bias the clinician toward an epilepsy diagnosis
Serial sodium monitoring showing normalization without resolution of seizure-like events provides additional evidence that the current episodes are not metabolic in origin
Psychosocial stressors (job loss, social isolation, grief) temporally correlate with symptom onset and are common precipitating factors in FND, though they are not required for the diagnosis

metadata.difficulty_description: Moderate difficulty due to multiple confounding factors: (1) history of a true prior hyponatremia-induced seizure creating expectation bias, (2) current mild hyponatremia serving as a red herring, (3) risperidone confounding prolactin interpretation, (4) comorbid schizophrenia risking diagnostic overshadowing, and (5) family history of epilepsy. However, positive functional signs on examination and EEG findings are present to guide diagnosis.
clinical_pearls: Hoover sign: involuntary hip extension on the contralateral side when testing hip flexion on the affected side — a reliable positive sign of functional weakness
Tremor entrainment: functional tremor changes frequency or is disrupted by voluntary rhythmic movements of another limb; also diminishes with distraction (mental arithmetic)
Eyes tightly closed during a seizure-like event is more common in PNES than in epileptic seizures
Post-event prolactin is unreliable in patients on dopamine-blocking antipsychotics like risperidone due to baseline hyperprolactinemia
Hyponatremia-induced seizures typically occur at sodium levels below 120 mEq/L; mild hyponatremia (130-135) rarely if ever causes seizures
Diagnostic overshadowing: the tendency to attribute all symptoms to an existing diagnosis (e.g., schizophrenia) and miss comorbid conditions like FND
